# UC-19.2.6 · HCI Replication Factor Policy Compliance (vSAN, Nutanix, HyperFlex, Azure Stack HCI)

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Configuration, Compliance &middot; **Wave:** Crawl &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We line up the written data-protection rules with what the storage system actually has set for each policy, container, or volume. We raise the alarm if something is on only one full copy, if the protection method is not what the team approved, or if objects never reached their policy. That way you are not finding out the hard way after a second failure during maintenance.*

---

## Description

Compares written replication and resiliency policy to declared configuration: vSAN storage-policy PFTT and failure-tolerance method (and per-object compliance from `vmware:vsan:object` when objects never reach their policy), Nutanix container `replication_factor`, HyperFlex cluster-wide `replication_factor`, and Azure Stack HCI `ResiliencySettingName` with `NumberOfDataCopies` / parity redundancy. Joins a governance `rf_policy` lookup to compute `rf_drift`, method allowance, and five-tier severity so teams catch RF=1 production volumes, stretched-label mistakes, and silent policy drift before a failure, distinct from live topology and runtime resiliency UCs.

## Value

A green dashboard on cluster health or successful rebuilds does not prove that every datastore was created under the org’s data-protection standard. A single RF=1 container, a vSAN object provisioned with force provisioning, or a HyperFlex cluster built at RF=2 when the standard demands RF=3 is a standing audit and recovery-capacity gap. This UC turns the written `min_required_rf` and `allowed_resiliency_methods` into a daily evidence table, so storage, app owners, and risk use one vocabulary for copy count, erasure class, and compliance class, and you do not learn about a mis-provisioned production volume only when the second failure lands during a rebuild window.

## Implementation

Create `index=hci` (or split per vendor), HEC token, `lookups/rf_policy.csv` with one row per governed policy/container/volume name per cluster, and a wrapper app for `props.conf` / `transforms.conf`. Run the Python 3 and PowerShell pollers on your schedule. Save the main search as a scheduled report every 30 minutes (or 5 for tight change control). Wire critical rows to PagerDuty, ServiceNow, Slack, and email as in Step 4. If you have ITSI, add KPIs for compliance score and non-compliant object count per cluster. Ensure RBAC on the lookup because it contains owner email and business_service.

## Evidence

Saved search `ss_uc_19_2_6_hci_rf_policy_compliance`, versioned `lookups/rf_policy.csv` with approver, and optional weekly CSV export to an `evidence_hci` index with retention that matches your control framework.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

In a lab, ingest vSAN `storagepolicy` and `object` events where a production-class lookup row has `min_required_rf=3` and `pftt=1` (mapped `actual_rf=2` as mirror), and/or `nonCompliant` objects for that `policy_id`; the saved search should return `rf_drift>0` or `non_compliant_object_count>0` and at least `high` or `critical` in `severity_bucket` (critical when production `non_compliant_object_count>0` or `actual_rf=1`) within the scheduled window.

### Negative scenario

Set `compliance_class=test` with `min_required_rf=1` in the lookup and ingest RF=1 container events; the row should be `low` or informational, and setting `change_freeze_until` to a future time should not upgrade severity. For vSAN, ingest only `notApplicable` object compliance; `non_compliant_object_count` should stay zero and must not single-handedly create `critical` when `crit_rf1=0`.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
The policy-versus-reality gap: a vSAN storage policy can list PFTT=2, but a specific object that was created during a node-down window with `force_provisioning` enabled can land with fewer active components than the policy’s steady-state. That is still a policy-compliance story, not a CPU spike; it is why this UC also reads `vmware:vsan:object` in addition to `vmware:vsan:storagepolicy`. The gap between what the control plane promises and what each object’s `current_compliance` field reports is the same class of error as a Nutanix “RF=2 in Prism” that still has cold data on an EC profile without your awareness — here we anchor on declared RF and on explicit non-complying objects, not on transient rebuild queue depth.
Why RF=1 in production is a critical finding: with a single full copy, any one disk, node, or rack fault that takes the only replica offline causes immediate unavailability and often unrecoverable loss if another fault overlaps maintenance. Most organisations forbid RF=1 for any tier tagged production in the `rf_policy` lookup. Why RF=2 vs RF=3 trade-off matters: RF=2 (two full copies) is roughly 50% raw efficiency on mirrors; RF=3 is roughly 33% but can survive a second failure during a rebuild window that RF=2 cannot. The UC encodes the standard in `min_required_rf`, not a universal moral rule, so a finance-approved RF=2 cluster can still be “ok” if the lookup says so. HyperFlex cluster-wide RF is chosen at build time: if the cluster is RF=2 and your written standard is RF=3, there is no in-place “flip switch” — you are documenting an approved exception, rebuilding, or living with a standing non-compliance, and this UC is where that line item becomes visible. Azure Stack HCI S2D: mirror and parity serve different IOPS and capacity trade-offs; heavy random write workloads on parity can be a performance trap even when parity is numerically “safe.” This UC is about resiliency class and method matching policy, not IOPS, but the primer below names MAP and parity so triage can route to performance siblings. Compliance versus availability: RF policy checks answer “did we create and keep the right resiliency contract?”; UC-19.2.16 answers “is the current fault-tolerance and domain layout what we think it is in real time?” — both are needed. Lookup `lookups/rf_policy.csv` (transform `rf_policy`) columns: `cluster_name` (string as shown in vCenter/Prism), `policy_or_container_name` (vSAN policy name, Nutanix container name, Azure volume FriendlyName, or a HyperFlex cluster display name when a single row covers the whole cluster), `policy_scope` (`cluster`, `container`, `policy`, `volume` — documentation for auditors), `min_required_rf` (integer: interpret as minimum acceptable effective copy/parity class after your mapping in Step 2), `allowed_resiliency_methods` (comma list such as `RAID-1,RAID-5` or `Mirror,Parity` for S2D), `business_service`, `owner_email`, `change_freeze_until` (ISO-8601 or date; empty if none; used to demote non-life-safety severities), `compliance_class` (`production`, `dev`, `test`, and any internal labels you already use in CMDB).
RF policy primer: vSphere PFTT 0 means no user failures tolerated (treat as RF=1 for mirror-style mapping in this UC’s SPL), 1 is the common RAID-1 FTM with one failure tolerated, 2 requires a larger node count, 3 is rare and host-heavy. vSAN FTM: RAID-1 (mirror) trades capacity for write latency; RAID-5 and RAID-6 erasure require minimum host counts (4+ and 6+ respectively for common layouts). Nutanix RF2 is the default “survive one component failure” posture; RF3 is for five or more nodes and two simultaneous failure tolerance in many designs; EC is often scoped to performance or capacity policies outside this UC unless you add fields. HyperFlex: cluster RF can be only 2 or 3 and is not changed without cluster rebuild. Azure Stack HCI: two-way and three-way mirrors, dual parity, and mirror-accelerated parity (MAP) each map to `ResiliencySettingName` and the mirror copy count in `Get-VirtualDisk` output; parity rows should use the lookup to allow `Parity` and set `min_required_rf` in line with your security baseline, not a naive mirror count.

### Step 1 — Configure data collection
vSphere vSAN: on a management host, install Python 3 with only the standard library. Use `ssl.create_default_context()` for TLS to vCenter, `urllib.request` for `POST /rest/com/sessions` (and refresh on HTTP 401), and JSON bodies for the `vmware-api-session-id` header on `GET` calls. Call BOTH `.../api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/storage-policies` (or the equivalent storage policy endpoint your vCenter build supports) and `.../api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/objects` with a page `page`/`count` or `offset` pattern until an empty page returns — large clusters (10,000+ objects) may take minutes; cap per-page size to avoid 504s, sleep briefly on 429, treat HTTP 500 as a possible vSAN services restart and back off before retrying the same cluster, and on other 5xx responses retry with capped exponential sleep. For each `storage-policies` response line, HEC to `sourcetype=vmware:vsan:storagepolicy` with `pftt`, `sftt`, `failure_tolerance_method`, `force_provisioning`, `applied_to_objects_count`, `policy_id`, and `cluster_name` from your inventory join. For each object page, emit `sourcetype=vmware:vsan:object` with `object_id`, `object_type`, `policy_id`, and `current_compliance`. In `props.conf` on the HEC input: `SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false`, `TRUNCATE = 1048576`, and either `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json` on the parsing tier or a `KV_MODE = json` stanza. Index both sourcetypes into the same `index=hci` (or your split) to keep the join cheap.
Nutanix: deploy `Splunk_TA_nutanix` 5076 to a heavy forwarder or a dedicated data collection VM with outbound HTTPS to port 9440. Enable the modular inputs for `nutanix:storage_container` and, if you track pool baselines, `nutanix:storage_pool`. In `local/inputs.conf`, set a 5–15 minute `interval` for containers (RF changes are rare, but a mis-click is worth catching within a maintenance window) and a longer interval for pools if the TA supports split schedules. In `local/props.conf`, do not clobber the TA’s `FIELDALIAS`; add your own app for calculated fields. Verify the incoming field list with `| fieldsummary` on one container name that you also see in Prism.
Cisco HyperFlex: with a read-only service account, poll `.../rest/clusters/{id}` (path exact to your HX release) to emit `sourcetype=cisco:hyperflex:cluster` with `replication_factor` and identification fields; optionally poll per-datastore resources to `cisco:hyperflex:datastore` if you need datastore labels for dashboards. HEC the JSON as single-line events; handle 401/429 like the vSphere poller.
Azure Stack HCI: create a gMSA or managed account that can run `Get-VirtualDisk` and, if required, `Get-StoragePool | Select FriendlyName, ResiliencySettingNameDefault` on a management node. A scheduled task every 15–30 minutes runs PowerShell, builds a compact object list per volume with `FriendlyName`, `ResiliencySettingName`, `NumberOfDataCopies`, `FaultDomainAwareness`, `PhysicalDiskRedundancy`, and posts JSON via HEC to `sourcetype=azure_stack_hci:volume` with `index = hci` and the same HEC token as other HCI streams (separate if your security model requires). Map cluster identity into `cluster_name` with a static prefix if you have multiple S2D clusters on one host.
`inputs.conf` and `props.conf` summary: one HEC global stanza per token; per-sourcetype stanzas name `TIME_FORMAT` if `_time` is in the body; for all JSON HCI feeds, set `SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false` and a large `TRUNCATE`. `transforms.conf` defines `rf_policy` = `LOOKUP-rf_policy` to `lookups/rf_policy.csv` with `case_sensitive_match = false` if you standardise on lower-case file keys. Schedule: 30 minutes is sufficient for most organisations because RF and storage policies change rarely; 5 minutes is justified when change control is very tight for storage admins or during large migrations. One emphasized phrase for this step: HEC back-pressure — if object enumeration runs long, run it as a separate Python process from the lightweight storage-policy poll so a stuck objects crawl does not block policy freshness.

### Step 2 — Create the search and alert
The saved search (canonical SPL) is the one in the `spl` field, repeated here for the operator manual. Fenced for copy-paste. All disjunctions in the base `where` use uppercase OR; inner predicates in `eval` use uppercase AND. The `comment` macro is the first token on the first line. One emphasized phrase: non-compliant object join — the subsearch on `vmware:vsan:object` is the detector for “policy says PFTT=2, object never met it,” separate from a healthy cluster-wide banner.

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.6 — RF policy compliance: vSAN PFTT/FTM, Nutanix container RF, HyperFlex cluster RF, Azure Stack S2D; rf_policy; vSAN object non-compliance; multisearch; uppercase AND/OR; earliest=-2h@h.")`
| multisearch
    [ search (index=hci OR index=vmware) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:storagepolicy" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name_cluster, cluster, vsphere_cluster)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(policy_name, policyName, name, policy_label)
      | eval policy_id=coalesce(policy_id, policyId, id, policy_uuid)
      | eval pftt=tonumber(coalesce(pftt, primary_failures_to_tolerate, pftt_value, PFTT, "0"), 10)
      | eval sftt=tonumber(coalesce(sftt, secondary_failures_to_tolerate, "0"), 10)
      | eval ftml=lower(tostring(coalesce(failure_tolerance_method, failureToleranceMethod, ftm, "RAID-1")))
      | eval actual_method=case(like(ftml, "%raid-5%") OR ftml="raid-5" OR ftml="erasure-5", "RAID-5", like(ftml, "%raid-6%") OR ftml="raid-6" OR ftml="erasure-6", "RAID-6", like(ftml, "%map%"), "MAP", like(ftml, "%mirror%"), "Mirror", like(ftml, "%parity%"), "Parity", 1=1, upper(trim(tostring(coalesce(failure_tolerance_method, failureToleranceMethod, "RAID-1")))))
      | eval _vsan_rf=if(like(actual_method, "RAID-5") OR like(actual_method, "RAID-6"), 2+tonumber(sftt, 10), 1 + pftt)
      | eval actual_rf=_vsan_rf
      | eval vendor="vmware" ]
    [ search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:storage_container" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name_cluster, cluster)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(container_name, containerName, name, container_uuid)
      | eval policy_id=""
      | eval rfn=tonumber(coalesce(replication_factor, replicationFactor, oplog_replication_factor, "0"), 10)
      | eval actual_rf=rfn
      | eval actual_method=if(rfn=3, "RF3", if(rfn=2, "RF2", "RF1"))
      | eval sftt=0
      | eval vendor="nutanix" ]
    [ search (index=hci OR index=cisco) sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, cluster, hx_cluster_name)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(name, cluster_name, clusterName, "cluster")
      | eval policy_id=""
      | eval rhx=tonumber(coalesce(replication_factor, hx_replication_factor, "0"), 10)
      | eval actual_rf=rhx
      | eval actual_method=if(rhx=3, "RF3", if(rhx=2, "RF2", "RF1"))
      | eval sftt=0
      | eval vendor="cisco" ]
    [ search (index=hci OR index=microsoft) sourcetype="azure_stack_hci:volume" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name_cluster, hci_cluster)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(friendlyName, FriendlyName, volume_name, name)
      | eval policy_id=""
      | eval rsn=coalesce(resiliency_setting_name, ResiliencySettingName, "Mirror")
      | eval ndc=tonumber(coalesce(number_of_data_copies, NumberOfDataCopies, num_data_copies, "2"), 10)
      | eval pdr=tonumber(coalesce(physical_disk_redundancy, PhysicalDiskRedundancy, "0"), 10)
      | eval _az_rf=case(like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%parity%"), 2 + pdr, 1=1, ndc)
      | eval actual_rf=_az_rf
      | eval actual_method=case(like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%map%"), "MAP", like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%parity%"), "Parity", like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%mirror%"), "Mirror", 1=1, tostring(rsn))
      | eval sftt=0
      | eval vendor="microsoft" ]
| eval cluster_name=if(isnotnull(cluster_name) AND len(tostring(cluster_name))>0, tostring(cluster_name), "UNKNOWN")
| where cluster_name!="UNKNOWN" AND ( vendor="vmware" OR vendor="nutanix" OR vendor="cisco" OR vendor="microsoft" )
| stats latest(actual_rf) as actual_rf
    latest(actual_method) as actual_method
    latest(policy_id) as policy_id
    latest(sftt) as sftt
    max(_time) as _time
  by cluster_name, policy_or_container_name, vendor
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name policy_or_container_name
    [| inputlookup rf_policy
     | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, cluster, ClusterName)
     | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(policy_or_container_name, policy_name, container_name, object_name, volume_name)
     | fields + cluster_name, policy_or_container_name, policy_scope, min_required_rf, allowed_resiliency_methods, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, compliance_class ]
| join type=left max=0 policy_id
    [ search (index=hci OR index=vmware) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:object" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval policy_id=coalesce(policy_id, policyId, id)
      | eval cc=lower(tostring(current_compliance))
      | where len(tostring(policy_id))>0 AND ( cc!="compliant" AND !like(cc, "%notapplic%"))
      | stats count as non_compliant_object_count by policy_id ]
| fillnull value=0 non_compliant_object_count
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name policy_id
    [ search (index=hci OR index=vmware) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:storagepolicy" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval policy_id=coalesce(policy_id, policyId, id)
      | eval fp=if(lower(tostring(coalesce(force_provisioning, forceProv, "false"))) IN ("1", "true", "t", "yes"), 1, 0)
      | stats latest(fp) as force_provisioning_flag by cluster_name, policy_id ]
| fillnull value=0 force_provisioning_flag
| eval min_required_rf=tonumber(tostring(min_required_rf), 10)
| eval rf_drift=if(isnotnull(min_required_rf), min_required_rf - actual_rf, null())
| eval ams=if(isnotnull(allowed_resiliency_methods), tostring(allowed_resiliency_methods), "*")
| eval method_compliant=if(like(lower(ams), "%"+lower(tostring(actual_method))+"%") OR ams="*" OR len(ams)=0, 1, 0)
| eval cfti=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z")
| eval cft2=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval cft3=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| eval cft4=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval freeze_active=if(isnotnull(cfti) OR isnotnull(cft2) OR isnotnull(cft3) OR isnotnull(cft4), if(now()<coalesce(cfti, cft2, cft3, cft4, 0), 1, 0), 0)
| eval is_prod=if(lower(tostring(compliance_class)) IN ("production", "prod", "p1"), 1, 0)
| eval crit_rf1=if(is_prod=1 AND actual_rf=1, 1, 0)
| eval crit_obj=if(is_prod=1 AND non_compliant_object_count>0, 1, 0)
| eval sev0=case(
    crit_rf1=1, "critical",
    crit_obj=1, "critical",
    ( is_prod=1 AND ( rf_drift>0 OR method_compliant=0 ) ), "high",
    ( ( is_prod=0 OR isnull(is_prod) ) AND ( rf_drift>0 OR method_compliant=0 ) AND crit_rf1=0 AND crit_obj=0 ), "medium",
    ( tolower(tostring(compliance_class)) IN ("test", "dev", "lab") AND ( actual_rf<2 ) ), "low",
    ( isnotnull(rf_drift) AND rf_drift<=0 AND method_compliant=1 AND ( non_compliant_object_count=0 OR isnull(non_compliant_object_count) ) ), "ok",
    1=1, "low" )
| eval severity_bucket=if( freeze_active=1 AND sev0!="critical" AND crit_rf1=0 AND crit_obj=0, case( sev0="high", "medium", sev0="medium", "low", sev0="low", "ok", 1=1, sev0 ), if( crit_rf1=1 OR crit_obj=1, "critical", sev0) )
| table _time, cluster_name, vendor, policy_or_container_name, actual_rf, min_required_rf, actual_method, allowed_resiliency_methods, rf_drift, non_compliant_object_count, compliance_class, severity_bucket, freeze_active, business_service, owner_email, force_provisioning_flag, method_compliant
| sort 0 - rf_drift, - non_compliant_object_count, cluster_name
```

Search walkthrough in plain English: the comment line ties scheduler logs to this UC id. The `multisearch` command unions four parallel `search` arms (vSAN storage policy, Nutanix container, HyperFlex cluster, Azure volume) so each sourcetype keeps its own field names until the common `eval` pass, with `earliest=-2h@h` on every arm. The long `coalesce` block normalises camelCase and snake_case for cluster and policy or container names so the lookup join works even after a TA upgrade. The `eval` chain derives `actual_rf` per vendor: for vSphere it starts from PFTT plus one for classic RAID-1 style mapping; for erasure it adds `sftt` where you need stretch tolerance in the same integer space as your `min_required_rf` definitions. Nutanix and HyperFlex read the numeric `replication_factor` straight from the TA and REST JSON. Azure maps mirrors from `NumberOfDataCopies` and maps parity to `2 + PhysicalDiskRedundancy` in the same integer space your governance team must document. The `stats` collapses to one row per `cluster_name`, `policy_or_container_name`, and `vendor` so double polls do not double counts. The first `inputlookup` join is the heart of the compliance story: you cannot drift what you did not document. The subsearch on `vmware:vsan:object` counts only objects whose `current_compliance` is not compliant and is not the “not applicable” sentinel, so transients in unrelated namespaces do not dominate. A second `join` adds `force_provisioning` so triage can separate a policy that allows thin provisioning in emergencies from a steady non-compliant set of objects. `rf_drift` is `min_required_rf` minus `actual_rf`, with positive values meaning under-protected. `method_compliant` is a simple substring test against the comma list in the lookup, which you can tighten in your org with `match` and explicit tokens if you fear substring collisions. The `case` for `severity_bucket` enforces: critical for RF=1 in production, critical also when non-compliant vSAN objects exist in production, high for under-protection or method mismatch in production, medium for the same in non-prod, low for test noise, and ok when all gates pass. The freeze block demotes only non-critical severities, and the inner `if` reasserts that RF=1 in production is never downgraded. The final `table` is the one you paste into dashboards; `sort` puts the riskiest drifts and object counts on top. Wire alert actions: PagerDuty for `severity_bucket` in (`critical`, `high`) on production, ServiceNow P1 for `actual_rf=1` with `compliance_class` production, P2 for `high` rows, Slack for `medium`, email for `low`.

### Step 3 — Validate
(1) VMware vSphere: Home → Policies and Profiles → VM Storage Policies → open each policy → Rules tab: read PFTT, FTM, and stretch settings side-by-side with your Splunk `pftt` and `actual_method` fields. (2) vSphere: cluster → Monitor → vSAN → Capacity → Virtual Objects or Object browser: find objects tied to a policy and confirm `compliance` matches `current_compliance` in Splunk for a sample. (3) `esxcli vsan policy getdefault` on a host — compare default PFTT to your lookup default row if you model defaults separately. (4) Nutanix Prism: Storage → Storage containers → each container: Replication Factor. (5) `ncli container ls` on CVM: confirm container names and RF against `policy_or_container_name` and `actual_rf`. (6) HyperFlex: HX Connect → System or Cluster summary: note cluster RF. (7) `stcli cluster info` (software-dependent path) for RF text that must match the REST poller. (8) Windows Admin Center on Azure Stack HCI: Volumes: `ResiliencySettingName` and layout. (9) PowerShell: `Get-VirtualDisk | Select FriendlyName, ResiliencySettingName, NumberOfDataCopies, PhysicalDiskRedundancy` and `Get-StoragePool | Select FriendlyName, ResiliencySettingNameDefault`. (10) cURL: vCenter `curl -k -H "vmware-api-session-id: $ID" https://$VC/api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/$CLUSTER_ID/storage-policies` and `.../objects` with pagination; Nutanix `https://$CVM:9440/PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/storage_containers/`, HyperFlex your documented `https://$HXIP/rest/clusters/`, Microsoft Graph/ARM is optional for HCI metadata, but the volume row should match `Get-VirtualDisk` first. (11) If UI shows a newer policy and Splunk shows the old: check poll lag, a stuck objects crawl, or a vSAN service restart. (12) If Nutanix shows “destroying” for a container, expect odd TA snapshots — validate after completion. (13) If S2D is rebalancing, parity volumes can briefly list transitional states; compare a second run 30 minutes later before opening a P1. (14) If only Splunk shows RF=1 and UI shows RF=2, you are probably mapping `replicationFactor` to the wrong JSON path in `coalesce` — run one `fieldsummary` on a known-good event.

### Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: row 1, two single values — `distinct_count` of `cluster_name` with `compliance_class` production and `actual_rf=1`, and a second single value of rows with `non_compliant_object_count>0`. Row 2, main table (same columns as the saved search `table`). Row 3, a matrix of `vendor` and `actual_method` (or `ResiliencySettingName` family) to spot accidental RAID-5 adoption on latency-sensitive classes. Row 4, `timechart` of count by `severity_bucket` to show drift trends after change windows. Alert routing: PagerDuty on `severity_bucket="critical"` (RF=1 or non-compliant objects in production as defined in Step 2), ServiceNow P1 for any production-class `actual_rf=1` line, P2 for `high`, Slack to `#hci-storage` for `medium`, email digests for `low` and for weekly compliance summaries. ITSI: entity per `cluster_name` (or per business_service if you model services first) with KPI `compliance_score = 100 * count(eval(severity_bucket="ok")) / count` over the day, a KPI for `max(rf_drift)` by day, and a KPI for `sum(non_compliant_object_count)` to catch object-level gaps. Runbooks: “RF=1 in production” — open an emergency change, enumerate VMs on that container or policy, and migrate to an RF2 or RF3 container; include backup verification. “Method drift to RAID-5 on a write-heavy vSAN class” — collect vSAN IOPS and latency, compare to RAID-1 class, and plan migration. “Non-compliant vSAN objects with force_provisioning in an incident” — open a “policy re-apply and resync” after nodes are healthy, and document why force provisioning was required.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting
(a) Empty result table: verify HEC token, indexer connectivity, and that the collection windows include events — `index=hci | stats count by sourcetype` should show all four. For vSphere, confirm the REST session is not 401; print `splunkd.log` on the poller. For multi-tenant vCenters, confirm `project_id` or vCenter instance in your poller’s configuration matches the events you think you are looking at. (b) `actual_rf` is always null: your `sourcetype` values differ from the four strings in the `eval`, or a field is nested — run `| fieldsummary actual_rf, replication_factor, pftt` and extend the `coalesce` list. (c) `non_compliant_object_count` is always zero: pagination may stop early, or the service account lacks `Vsan` views — compare object event count in Splunk to the object count in the vSphere UI. (d) HyperFlex at RF=2 with a standard that demands RF=3: treat as a capital decision — you cannot retune RF without rebuild; either update the lookup with a signed exception or schedule cluster rebuild. (e) Azure shows `ResiliencySettingName` Mirror with `NumberOfDataCopies=2`: that is a two-way mirror, equivalent to two full copies, not a bug; a three-way mirror would show 3. (f) Nutanix container in Splunk says RF2 but `ncli` says RF3: API version skew or cached TA output — re-pull from Prism and compare the raw JSON for that container. (g) A freeze row that demotes a critical RF=1: review `severity_bucket` `eval` — `crit_rf1` should force `critical` regardless; if not, a typo in the `is_prod` test is the usual root cause. (h) A vSAN policy with `force_provisioning` always has non-compliant objects: treat the policy and the object feed differently — the flag column exists so dashboards can filter to “operational, not just mis-tuned” rows. (i) vSAN is per-VM or per-vDisk policy, Nutanix is per-container: the unit of `policy_or_container_name` differs — document a dashboard subtitle so executives do not compare unlike rows. (j) MAP S2D volumes: WAC and `Get-VirtualDisk` can show tier-specific resiliency; this UC’s primary `actual_method` is the volume’s top-level resiliency — split tier checks if you require both in scope. (k) PowerStore or other HCI: map StoreOS protect policies in a new sourcetype and add one more arm before the `stats`, keeping the `rf_policy` story the same. (l) Splunk `join` result explosion: if `cluster_name+policy_id` is not unique in the force-provision join, switch to `stats latest` only where uniqueness holds, or pre-dedup in a subsearch.



## SPL

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.6 — RF policy compliance: vSAN PFTT/FTM, Nutanix container RF, HyperFlex cluster RF, Azure Stack S2D; rf_policy; vSAN object non-compliance; multisearch; uppercase AND/OR; earliest=-2h@h.")`
| multisearch
    [ search (index=hci OR index=vmware) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:storagepolicy" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name_cluster, cluster, vsphere_cluster)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(policy_name, policyName, name, policy_label)
      | eval policy_id=coalesce(policy_id, policyId, id, policy_uuid)
      | eval pftt=tonumber(coalesce(pftt, primary_failures_to_tolerate, pftt_value, PFTT, "0"), 10)
      | eval sftt=tonumber(coalesce(sftt, secondary_failures_to_tolerate, "0"), 10)
      | eval ftml=lower(tostring(coalesce(failure_tolerance_method, failureToleranceMethod, ftm, "RAID-1")))
      | eval actual_method=case(like(ftml, "%raid-5%") OR ftml="raid-5" OR ftml="erasure-5", "RAID-5", like(ftml, "%raid-6%") OR ftml="raid-6" OR ftml="erasure-6", "RAID-6", like(ftml, "%map%"), "MAP", like(ftml, "%mirror%"), "Mirror", like(ftml, "%parity%"), "Parity", 1=1, upper(trim(tostring(coalesce(failure_tolerance_method, failureToleranceMethod, "RAID-1")))))
      | eval _vsan_rf=if(like(actual_method, "RAID-5") OR like(actual_method, "RAID-6"), 2+tonumber(sftt, 10), 1 + pftt)
      | eval actual_rf=_vsan_rf
      | eval vendor="vmware" ]
    [ search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:storage_container" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name_cluster, cluster)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(container_name, containerName, name, container_uuid)
      | eval policy_id=""
      | eval rfn=tonumber(coalesce(replication_factor, replicationFactor, oplog_replication_factor, "0"), 10)
      | eval actual_rf=rfn
      | eval actual_method=if(rfn=3, "RF3", if(rfn=2, "RF2", "RF1"))
      | eval sftt=0
      | eval vendor="nutanix" ]
    [ search (index=hci OR index=cisco) sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, cluster, hx_cluster_name)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(name, cluster_name, clusterName, "cluster")
      | eval policy_id=""
      | eval rhx=tonumber(coalesce(replication_factor, hx_replication_factor, "0"), 10)
      | eval actual_rf=rhx
      | eval actual_method=if(rhx=3, "RF3", if(rhx=2, "RF2", "RF1"))
      | eval sftt=0
      | eval vendor="cisco" ]
    [ search (index=hci OR index=microsoft) sourcetype="azure_stack_hci:volume" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name_cluster, hci_cluster)
      | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(friendlyName, FriendlyName, volume_name, name)
      | eval policy_id=""
      | eval rsn=coalesce(resiliency_setting_name, ResiliencySettingName, "Mirror")
      | eval ndc=tonumber(coalesce(number_of_data_copies, NumberOfDataCopies, num_data_copies, "2"), 10)
      | eval pdr=tonumber(coalesce(physical_disk_redundancy, PhysicalDiskRedundancy, "0"), 10)
      | eval _az_rf=case(like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%parity%"), 2 + pdr, 1=1, ndc)
      | eval actual_rf=_az_rf
      | eval actual_method=case(like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%map%"), "MAP", like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%parity%"), "Parity", like(lower(tostring(rsn)), "%mirror%"), "Mirror", 1=1, tostring(rsn))
      | eval sftt=0
      | eval vendor="microsoft" ]
| eval cluster_name=if(isnotnull(cluster_name) AND len(tostring(cluster_name))>0, tostring(cluster_name), "UNKNOWN")
| where cluster_name!="UNKNOWN" AND ( vendor="vmware" OR vendor="nutanix" OR vendor="cisco" OR vendor="microsoft" )
| stats latest(actual_rf) as actual_rf
    latest(actual_method) as actual_method
    latest(policy_id) as policy_id
    latest(sftt) as sftt
    max(_time) as _time
  by cluster_name, policy_or_container_name, vendor
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name policy_or_container_name
    [| inputlookup rf_policy
     | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, cluster, ClusterName)
     | eval policy_or_container_name=coalesce(policy_or_container_name, policy_name, container_name, object_name, volume_name)
     | fields + cluster_name, policy_or_container_name, policy_scope, min_required_rf, allowed_resiliency_methods, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, compliance_class ]
| join type=left max=0 policy_id
    [ search (index=hci OR index=vmware) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:object" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval policy_id=coalesce(policy_id, policyId, id)
      | eval cc=lower(tostring(current_compliance))
      | where len(tostring(policy_id))>0 AND ( cc!="compliant" AND !like(cc, "%notapplic%"))
      | stats count as non_compliant_object_count by policy_id ]
| fillnull value=0 non_compliant_object_count
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name policy_id
    [ search (index=hci OR index=vmware) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:storagepolicy" earliest=-2h@h
      | eval policy_id=coalesce(policy_id, policyId, id)
      | eval fp=if(lower(tostring(coalesce(force_provisioning, forceProv, "false"))) IN ("1", "true", "t", "yes"), 1, 0)
      | stats latest(fp) as force_provisioning_flag by cluster_name, policy_id ]
| fillnull value=0 force_provisioning_flag
| eval min_required_rf=tonumber(tostring(min_required_rf), 10)
| eval rf_drift=if(isnotnull(min_required_rf), min_required_rf - actual_rf, null())
| eval ams=if(isnotnull(allowed_resiliency_methods), tostring(allowed_resiliency_methods), "*")
| eval method_compliant=if(like(lower(ams), "%"+lower(tostring(actual_method))+"%") OR ams="*" OR len(ams)=0, 1, 0)
| eval cfti=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z")
| eval cft2=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval cft3=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| eval cft4=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval freeze_active=if(isnotnull(cfti) OR isnotnull(cft2) OR isnotnull(cft3) OR isnotnull(cft4), if(now()<coalesce(cfti, cft2, cft3, cft4, 0), 1, 0), 0)
| eval is_prod=if(lower(tostring(compliance_class)) IN ("production", "prod", "p1"), 1, 0)
| eval crit_rf1=if(is_prod=1 AND actual_rf=1, 1, 0)
| eval crit_obj=if(is_prod=1 AND non_compliant_object_count>0, 1, 0)
| eval sev0=case(
    crit_rf1=1, "critical",
    crit_obj=1, "critical",
    ( is_prod=1 AND ( rf_drift>0 OR method_compliant=0 ) ), "high",
    ( ( is_prod=0 OR isnull(is_prod) ) AND ( rf_drift>0 OR method_compliant=0 ) AND crit_rf1=0 AND crit_obj=0 ), "medium",
    ( tolower(tostring(compliance_class)) IN ("test", "dev", "lab") AND ( actual_rf<2 ) ), "low",
    ( isnotnull(rf_drift) AND rf_drift<=0 AND method_compliant=1 AND ( non_compliant_object_count=0 OR isnull(non_compliant_object_count) ) ), "ok",
    1=1, "low" )
| eval severity_bucket=if( freeze_active=1 AND sev0!="critical" AND crit_rf1=0 AND crit_obj=0, case( sev0="high", "medium", sev0="medium", "low", sev0="low", "ok", 1=1, sev0 ), if( crit_rf1=1 OR crit_obj=1, "critical", sev0) )
| table _time, cluster_name, vendor, policy_or_container_name, actual_rf, min_required_rf, actual_method, allowed_resiliency_methods, rf_drift, non_compliant_object_count, compliance_class, severity_bucket, freeze_active, business_service, owner_email, force_provisioning_flag, method_compliant
| sort 0 - rf_drift, - non_compliant_object_count, cluster_name
```

## Visualization

Row 1: two single values — count of unique clusters with `actual_rf=1` where `compliance_class` is production, and count of policy rows with `non_compliant_object_count>0`. Row 2: table of the saved search (sort by `rf_drift` then `non_compliant_object_count`). Row 3: matrix or column chart of vendor by `actual_method`. Row 4: timechart of count by `severity_bucket`. Optional: panel filtered to `force_provisioning_flag=1` for vSAN to separate intentional thin provisioning from steady-state gaps.

## Known False Positives

A brand-new vSAN object can show `nonCompliant` until components fully instantiate; two consecutive polls with growth in `compliant` count usually clears the alert without a runbook. During stretch or witness work, `sftt` and PFTT together can match policy while a transient API still lists some objects as non-compliant — compare Object Browser in vSphere. Nutanix `replication_factor` and `oplog_replication_factor` can differ by design; this UC uses `replication_factor` for data copies unless you extend the SPL. HyperFlex at RF=2 is valid for your standard but still flags if the lookup says RF=3: that is an intentional “accept risk” case, not a poller error. Parity and MAP S2D volumes often look “lower” in mirror-equivalent `actual_rf` math even when parity fault tolerance is correct — tune `min_required_rf` and `allowed_resiliency_methods` for your parity policy rows. Test clusters with RF=1 in `compliance_class=test` can appear as low but should not page if freeze and class logic are aligned. vSAN `notApplicable` objects are explicitly excluded from `non_compliant_object_count` to avoid swamping the join with iSCSI paths that never participate in the policy. After TA upgrades, a renamed JSON field can look like a breach until you extend the `coalesce` list; verify with one `fieldsummary` run before you silence anything.

## References

- [VMware — Manage vSphere Storage Policies and vSAN policies](https://techdocs.broadcom.com/us/en/vmware-cis/vsphere/vsphere-8-0/8-0-0/vsphere-storage/vsphere-storage-policy-based-management/managing-a-vsphere-storage-policy.html)
- [Nutanix — Storage containers and replication (Prism and RF)](https://portal.nutanix.com/page/documents/details?targetId=Web-Console-Guide-Prism:wc-storage-pools-containers-c.html)
- [Cisco — HyperFlex architecture: replication and data protection overview](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/solutions/collateral/hyperconverged-infrastructure/hyperconverged-infrastructure-software/hyperflex-software-software-agility-and-scale-for-multicloud-software.html)
- [Microsoft Learn — Azure Stack HCI: Storage resiliency and volumes (Storage Spaces Direct concepts)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure-stack/hci/concepts/strengthen-security-with-storage)
